text: The surgeon explores the abdominal cavity, visually assessing the location of the tumor situated 13 centimeters from the anal verge.
The surgeon separates the left natural attachment and lateral peritoneum, placing a gauze strip for guidance in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a medial lateral approach, creating tension to facilitate the incision of the peritoneum. As the peritoneum is incised, air enters the surgical field, enhancing visibility and clarity of the space.
The surgeon separates the talt space using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, taking care to preserve the visible super wire hypogastric plexus during the procedure.

Following the separation of the total space, the surgeon dissects the apical lymph nodes using a harmonic scalpel, carefully excising the tissue to prevent damage to the duodenum and avoid post-operative lymphatic fistula.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its root and proceeds to separate the attachment between the IMA and the left sympathetic nerve (LSN).
The surgeon extends the talt space further, revealing the previously placed gauze strip within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and left genital vessels. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and left colic artery (LCA) are ligated together.
The surgeon separates the retrorectal space while preserving the inferior hypogastric plexus. The recto sacrofasta is incised, allowing entry into the superior space of the levator ani muscle.
The surgeon carefully separates the lateral space of the rectum while preserving the pelvic plexus, ensuring the anatomical structures remain intact during the dissection.
The surgeon uses a five-centimeter silk thread to mark the location for excision, followed by the excision of the tumor-specific mesorectum.
The surgeon transects the rectum using a surgical stapler, followed by a visual inspection of the entire epigastric plexus.
The surgeon extracts the excised specimen through a small incision, completing the removal process.
The surgeon constructs an end-to-end anastomosis using a double stapling technique, followed by an ear leak test to confirm the integrity of the connection.
The surgeon places a pelvic drainage tube in the surgical site following the excision and separation procedures.
